body.p:
As a developer, you are often faced with the situation that there are several possible solutions to a particular problem, and you have to choose one of them.
UWE test footnote (26/40, callout Z): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
Here it is about finding a trade-offTrade-Off between often competing goals, as the following example shows. Consider an account:
body.pre: <konto nr="123"> <inhaber>alex</inhaber> <vorgang>456</vorgang> <eintrag art="soll" datum="2019-05-06">20.56</eintrag> <eintrag art="soll" datum="2019-02-21">4.73</eintrag> <eintrag art="haben" datum="2019-01-14">1.68</eintrag> <eintrag art="soll" datum="2019-09-17">6.45</eintrag> <eintrag art="haben" datum="2019-01-03">12.38</eintrag> [...] </konto>
body.p: There are now several ways to split the balance into two files soll.xml and haben.xml.

section.title: Loop
section.pre: <xsl:template match="/"> <xsl:result-document href="soll.xml"> <konto nr="{@nr}"> <inhaber><xsl:value-of select="inhaber"/></inhaber> <vorgang><xsl:value-of select="vorgang"/></vorgang> <xsl:for-each select="konto/eintrag"> <xsl:if test="@art='soll'"> <xsl:copy-of select="."/> </xsl:if> </xsl:for-each> </konto> </xsl:result-document> <xsl:result-document href="haben.xml"> [...] </xsl:template>
section.p: Here, the common fields for the account <inhaber> and <vorgang> are hard-coded, which would be fine in this simple example. But we actually want to avoid pull stylesheetsConceptsPush vs. Pull , cf. Push vs. Pull Stylesheets.

section.title: Tunnel Parameter
section.pre:
<xsl:template match="/"> <xsl:result-document href="soll.xml"> <xsl:apply-templates> <xsl:with-param name="is-soll" select="true()" tunnel="yes"/> </xsl:apply-templates> </xsl:result-document> <xsl:result-document href="haben.xml"> <xsl:apply-templates> <xsl:with-param name="is-soll" select="false()" tunnel="yes"/> </xsl:apply-templates> </xsl:result-document> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="node()|@*"> <xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*"/> </xsl:copy> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="eintrag"> <xsl:param name="is-soll" tunnel="yes"/> <xsl:choose> <xsl:when test="$is-soll and @art='soll'"> <xsl:copy-of select="."/> </xsl:when> <xsl:when test="not($is-soll) and @art='haben'"> <xsl:copy-of select="."/> </xsl:when> <xsl:otherwise/> </xsl:choose> </xsl:template>
section.p: Here, all nodes of the XML skeleton are copied via a default copy rule, i.e. the skeleton can be arbitrarily large, and we do not need to worry about it. In this respect, this solution is a step more generic than the one shown previously.XSLT ConstructsTunnel Parameter

We are even very flexible with regard to changes, since additional logic can easily be implemented in the entry template that spans all branches of the conditional statement, e.g. via a local variable that contains a calculation expression.

section.title: Mode Attribute
section.p: (Even) shorter is the approach using the mode attribute:XSLT ConstructsMode Attribute
section.pre:
<xsl:template match="/"> <xsl:result-document href="soll.xml"> <xsl:apply-templates mode="soll"/> </xsl:result-document> <xsl:result-document href="haben.xml"> <xsl:apply-templates mode="haben"/> </xsl:result-document> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="node()|@*" mode="#all"> <xsl:copy> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*" mode="#current"/> </xsl:copy> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="eintrag[@art='soll']" mode="haben"/> <xsl:template match="eintrag[@art='haben']" mode="soll"/>
section.p: The trick here is that in the respective other mode, precisely the selected nodes are not copied, which ultimately correctly splits the input into debit and credit. If we want to add further logic, we would have to restructure the templates a bit, which should not be a major issue.
The trade-offTrade-Off thus consists of the usually competing goals of reducing redundancy, i.e. keeping the source code as short and concise as possible, and easy extensibility in the future.
